questions.question: Select all that are true about how a fixed-size window of width k advances by one step.
choices: You subtract the element leaving the left edge and add the element entering the right edge.
The middle elements of the window are re-added from scratch on every slide.
The cost of one slide stays constant no matter how wide the window is.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A slide edits only the two boundary elements, so the middle is carried for free and the per-slide cost is constant regardless of window width.

questions.question: Select all that are true about how many times a full k-element sum is computed from scratch in the fixed-window max-sum solution.
choices: Exactly once, for the very first window.
Once for every window position.
k times in total.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Only the first window is summed honestly. Every later window is an O(1) repair of the two ends, not a rebuild.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the work done at each slide of a fixed-size window.
choices: It performs one subtraction for the element that leaves.
It performs one addition for the element that enters.
It performs one comparison against the best seen so far.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Each slide is exactly one subtraction, one addition, and one comparison, and none of that grows with the window width.

questions.question: Select all that are true about which element leaves a fixed-size window of width k when the right pointer sits at index r (with r at least k).
choices: The element at index r.
The element at index r minus 1.
The element at index 0 every time.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The element that leaves is the one k positions behind the right pointer, at index r minus k. None of the listed indices describes it, so None of the above is correct.

questions.question: Select all that are true about what the left boundary does in a variable-size window.
choices: It advances only to restore the window's validity rule after the right boundary broke it.
It advances to keep the window pinned at exactly size k.
It sorts the elements currently inside the window.
None of the above
questions.explanation: In a variable window the left boundary steps forward only to release elements until the window is legal again. The width is not fixed and nothing is sorted.

questions.question: Select all that are NOT true about why a variable-size window runs in O(n) despite its inner shrink loop.
choices: Each index is admitted once by the right boundary and released at most once by the left boundary.
Total pointer travel is bounded by about 2n.
The inner shrink loop runs a fixed three times per element.
It is linear only because the array is sorted first.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The first two statements are the true amortized argument. The shrink loop does not run a fixed number of times, and sorting is not required, so those two false statements are the NOT-true selections.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the 'smallest subarray with sum at least a target' window trick.
choices: It relies on all numbers being positive, so a wider window has a larger-or-equal sum.
The trick is safe because validity is monotonic in the window size.
Introducing a negative number breaks the monotonic promise, so you reach for prefix sums or a deque instead.
It works on any array regardless of the sign of the numbers.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Positivity makes validity monotonic in window size, which is what makes shrinking safe. A negative number breaks that promise, so the last claim is false.

questions.question: Select all that are true about which keywords hint at a contiguous-run sliding window.
choices: 'subarray' describes a contiguous run and hints at a sliding window.
'substring' describes a contiguous run and hints at a sliding window.
'subsequence' is contiguous and is a natural sliding-window fit.
None of the above
questions.explanation: 'subarray' and 'substring' are contiguous runs. A 'subsequence' is not contiguous, so a window cannot slide over it.
